QUESTION: Why do I have to fill all this in?
SHORT_DESCRIPTION: Why sellers must disclose property information and what happens if they don't.
MAIN_EXPLANATION:
When you sell a home, the buyer is making one of the largest financial decisions of their life. The questions you answer give them the information they need to make that decision safely — and your answers can form part of the binding sale contract.

You are legally responsible for your answers. Providing misleading information — even accidentally — can result in the buyer claiming compensation after the sale completes.

Leaving questions blank is not the same as saying 'not known.' Blanks cause delays and raise red flags. If you genuinely don't know something, say so explicitly.

The Three Golden Rules for Sellers:
• Answer everything — even if the answer is 'not known'
• Be truthful and complete — partial disclosure is almost as bad as none
• Tell your solicitor immediately if any answers change before completion
BUYER_GUIDANCE: The seller's disclosure is your starting point, not your safety net. Use it alongside an independent building survey, your solicitor's searches, and your own observations from viewings. If you receive any additional information about the property from the estate agent, seller, or online, tell your solicitor.
SELLER_GUIDANCE: Check your paperwork — not just your memory. Gather planning permissions, guarantees, warranties, and certificates before you start. If something changes after you have submitted your answers, tell your solicitor immediately.
KEY_POINTS:
• Sellers are legally responsible for their answers
• Misleading a buyer — even accidentally — can lead to compensation claims
• 'Not known' is acceptable; blank answers are not
• Tell your solicitor immediately if anything changes

[FOR SELLERS]

[FOR BUYERS]

QUESTION: Who should be answering these questions?
SHORT_DESCRIPTION: Who must complete and sign property disclosure — owners, executors, attorneys, and companies.
MAIN_EXPLANATION:
The legal owner of the property must answer — the person or people named on the Land Registry title or the title deeds.

• Standard owner: Complete it yourself as the registered owner
• Executor or Administrator: Selling on behalf of a deceased estate — gather information from people who knew the property
• Attorney: Completing under a power of attorney — must have valid legal authority to sell
• Trustee: Completing on behalf of a trust arrangement
• Company seller: A director or person authorised by a director completes the information

The Signing Rule: ALL sellers must sign. If two or more people own the property, every owner must sign. One person cannot sign on behalf of another without specific legal authority to do so.
BUYER_GUIDANCE: If the seller is an executor, attorney, or trustee, they may have limited personal knowledge of the property's history. Commission a thorough building survey and ask your solicitor about areas where the disclosure says 'not known.'
SELLER_GUIDANCE: Check who is named on the Land Registry title — those are the people who need to sign. If anyone named has died or lost capacity, tell your solicitor before you begin. If acting as executor, gather whatever paperwork exists and note where gaps are.
KEY_POINTS:
• Must be completed by the legal owner named on the Land Registry title
• ALL owners must sign — one cannot sign for another without legal authority
• Executors, attorneys, and trustees can complete it — but remain legally responsible
• When in doubt, ask your solicitor before starting

QUESTION: Who is responsible for your property boundaries?
SHORT_DESCRIPTION: Boundary ownership and maintenance responsibility — left, right, rear, and front.
MAIN_EXPLANATION:
Looking at your property from the road, you will be asked who owns or is responsible for maintaining the boundary features on each side. The options are: You / Your Neighbour / Shared / Not Known.

How to find out: Check your title deeds for boundary markers:
• A T-mark pointing inward toward your property = that boundary is your responsibility
• An H-mark = shared responsibility with the neighbour

If you cannot find clear documentation, 'Not Known' is a valid and honest answer. Do not guess.

Common misconceptions:
• A fence on the left does not automatically mean the left boundary is your responsibility
• Title plans show general boundary positions, not precise legal lines
• The presence of a fence does not indicate its ownership
BUYER_GUIDANCE: If you plan to erect a fence or build near a boundary, confirm which boundaries you own before exchange. The physical boundary you see at a viewing may not match the legal boundary on the title plan. Ask your solicitor to investigate if this matters to your plans.
SELLER_GUIDANCE: Check your title deeds before answering. If you are not sure, 'Not Known' is always better than a wrong guess that later causes a dispute.
KEY_POINTS:
• Check title deeds for T-marks and H-marks before answering
• 'Not Known' is always better than guessing
• Physical fences do not automatically indicate legal ownership
• Boundary disputes are one of the most common causes of neighbour conflicts

QUESTION: Have any of your property boundaries ever been moved or changed?
SHORT_DESCRIPTION: Boundary changes, land additions, and neighbour encroachments that must be disclosed.
MAIN_EXPLANATION:
You need to disclose if you are aware of:
• A fence, wall, hedge, or other boundary feature being moved
• Land bought from a neighbour and added to the property
• A neighbour building on or encroaching on any part of your property
• Any change in how physical boundaries relate to the title plan

This applies even if the change happened before your ownership — if you know about it, you must disclose it.

An undocumented boundary change can cause serious legal complications for a buyer. If the physical boundaries do not match the official Land Registry title plan, future disputes become harder to resolve.
BUYER_GUIDANCE: If a boundary change is disclosed, ask your solicitor to check whether it is properly reflected in the Land Registry title. Compare what you see physically during your viewing with the title plan. Any discrepancy should be flagged to your solicitor immediately.
SELLER_GUIDANCE: Even if a boundary change feels like ancient history, disclose it. Your solicitor can help you frame the answer appropriately. If you bought extra land from a neighbour, check whether it was properly registered at the time.
KEY_POINTS:
• Disclose any boundary changes you are aware of — even historic ones
• Changes that are not registered at Land Registry create legal uncertainty
• Physical and legal boundaries do not always match
• Your solicitor can advise on how to handle this in the sale
VIDEO_SCRIPT_FULL:

"Has a fence been moved? Has a neighbour built over your boundary? This is what this question is asking.

[WHY IT MATTERS]
Any boundary change that has not been officially recorded creates a legal grey area. The title plan may show one thing but the physical reality is different.

[WHAT TO DISCLOSE]
If you know about any boundary change — even decades ago, even not your doing — you need to say so.

[FOR BUYERS]
During your viewing, compare what you see physically with the title plan. If they don't match, tell your solicitor before exchange.

[CLOSE]

QUESTION: Does any part of your property overhang or extend beyond its boundary?
SHORT_DESCRIPTION: Overhanging eaves, gutters, underground extensions, and other features that cross the legal boundary.
MAIN_EXPLANATION:
Some buildings have physical elements that extend beyond the legal boundary. These are more common than people think. Examples include:
• Overhanging roof eaves projecting into the neighbour's airspace
• Gutters or pipes crossing the boundary line
• Underground cellars extending beneath a neighbouring property or public pavement
• Upper-storey rooms sitting above a neighbour's passageway

Features that have been in place for a long time — especially those mentioned in the title deeds — generally have established legal rights to remain. But they need to be properly documented so the buyer understands what they are taking on.
BUYER_GUIDANCE: An overhanging feature is not automatically a dealbreaker. What matters is whether the right to maintain it is properly established. Your solicitor will check whether the feature is mentioned in the title deeds or whether rights have been established through long use.
SELLER_GUIDANCE: Look carefully at your building from the outside. Do your eaves, gutters, or pipes cross into next door's airspace? If you are not sure, your surveyor or solicitor can help you check. Disclose anything you are aware of.
KEY_POINTS:
• Features crossing the boundary are more common than people think
• Long-established features usually have legal rights to remain
• They must be disclosed so they can be addressed in the legal documents
• Indemnity insurance may be available if documentation is missing

[IS THIS A PROBLEM?]
Usually not. Features that have been in place for a long time generally have established rights to stay. But they need to be documented.

QUESTION: Have you carried out building work near a shared boundary or wall?
SHORT_DESCRIPTION: Party Wall Act requirements, what notices are needed, and why missing documentation matters.
MAIN_EXPLANATION:
Certain building works near a shared boundary or party wall require you to give your neighbours formal written notice before you start. This is a legal requirement under the Party Wall etc. Act 1996.

When is notice required:
• Constructing a new wall on or at the boundary line
• Carrying out work to a shared wall — including extensions
• Excavating within 3-6 metres of a neighbouring building

Common projects that trigger this: loft conversions, rear extensions, basement excavations.

What notice looks like: A formal written notice sent to each affected neighbour. Any resulting agreement is documented in a Party Wall Award. Missing party wall notices for relevant work can raise questions about whether the work was done correctly.
BUYER_GUIDANCE: Party wall documentation shows that relevant building work was carried out with proper legal notice to neighbours. If significant work was done without the required notice, ask your solicitor to assess whether this creates any risk.
SELLER_GUIDANCE: Look through your files for any party wall notices sent to or received from neighbours, and any party wall award or agreement documents. If you had work done and cannot find notices, tell your solicitor — indemnity insurance may be the solution.
KEY_POINTS:
• The Party Wall Act requires formal written notice before certain works near shared boundaries
• Triggered by: extensions using shared walls, loft conversions, deep excavations
• All notices and party wall awards should be provided with the property information
• Missing notices can be covered by indemnity insurance in some cases
VIDEO_SCRIPT_FULL:
[INTRO]
"Planning permission isn't the only legal hurdle for home improvements. If you've done building work near a shared boundary, there's a separate legal process you may have needed to follow.

[WHAT IS IT?]
The Party Wall etc. Act 1996 requires you to give your neighbours formal written notice before certain works — extensions, loft conversions, deep excavations.

[FOR SELLERS]
Look through your paperwork. If you did any of these kinds of works, find those notices and include them.

[FOR BUYERS]
If major work was done without notices, your solicitor will advise whether indemnity insurance is appropriate.

[CLOSE]
Party wall notices: small piece of paperwork, potentially big importance."

QUESTION: Have there been any disputes or complaints about your property or a nearby property?
SHORT_DESCRIPTION: What must be disclosed about past and present disputes, including informal neighbourhood conflicts.
MAIN_EXPLANATION:
You must disclose any disputes or complaints — past or current. This is broader than most sellers expect. It includes:
• Formal legal disputes and solicitors' letters
• Complaints to the council about a neighbour (or from a neighbour about you)
• Arguments about noise, parking, access, boundaries, or trees
• Disputes about shared facilities or communal areas

Resolved disputes still need to be disclosed. Even if something was sorted out amicably years ago, if you are aware of it, you must say so.

Failing to disclose a known dispute that a buyer later discovers is one of the most common grounds for post-completion compensation claims.
BUYER_GUIDANCE: If disputes are disclosed, ask for full details: what happened, when, who was involved, and how it was resolved. Ask your solicitor whether the underlying issue could recur. Consider making your own enquiries with neighbours before exchange.
SELLER_GUIDANCE: Be honest, even if it feels uncomfortable. Your solicitor can help you phrase disclosures appropriately. Ask yourself: any formal complaints? Any argument involving a professional or the council? If yes, disclose it.
KEY_POINTS:
• Disclose ALL disputes — past or present, formal or informal
• Resolved disputes still need to be disclosed
• Noise, parking, access, boundary, and tree disputes all count
• Hiding a known dispute is a common ground for post-completion compensation claims

"This is the question that catches more sellers out than almost any other: have there been any disputes or complaints about your property — or a property nearby?

[HOW BROAD IS 'DISPUTE'?]

[THE RISK OF STAYING SILENT]
If a buyer discovers a dispute you knew about but didn't disclose — that's grounds for a compensation claim.

QUESTION: Have you received any official notices or communications about your property?
SHORT_DESCRIPTION: Council notices, planning applications, road proposals, and other formal communications that must be disclosed.
MAIN_EXPLANATION:
You should disclose any formal notices or official correspondence that might affect the property or the surrounding area, including:
• Planning applications from you or a neighbour
• Council proposals for road changes or new parking restrictions
• Compulsory purchase proposals or consultations
• Conservation area or listed building designation consultations
• Tree preservation orders being placed on trees
• Notices from utility companies about works in the area
• Enforcement notices from the local authority about any nearby property
• Highways notices about road adoption or improvements

Notices can arrive as printed letters, emails, or planning consultation leaflets.
BUYER_GUIDANCE: Even if nothing is disclosed, your solicitor's local authority search will pick up many relevant issues. However, searches have a time lag — very recent proposals may not appear. Check the local planning portal directly if you are concerned about nearby development.
SELLER_GUIDANCE: Go through your paperwork and emails. Have you received anything from the council, a neighbour's architect, a utility company, or the highways authority? Even planning consultation letters about developments nearby could be relevant.
KEY_POINTS:
• Disclose all formal notices and official correspondence received
• Includes planning applications, road proposals, utility notices, and enforcement actions
• Your solicitor's searches will catch many things — but not everything recent
• Check your files and emails before answering

[INTRO]
"This question is about official notices — formal communications you've received that might affect your property or the area.

[EXAMPLES]

[FOR SELLERS]
Check your files and your inbox. Even planning consultation leaflets count.

[FOR BUYERS]
Your solicitor's local authority search will catch a lot — but not the most recent proposals. Check the local planning portal directly if concerned.

[CLOSE]
Notices: easy to forget, important to disclose."

QUESTION: Has any building work been carried out at the property?
SHORT_DESCRIPTION: Planning permission, building regulations approval, and competent person certificates for home improvements.
MAIN_EXPLANATION:
Any significant changes to the property should be disclosed, including: extensions, loft conversions, garage conversions, conservatories, replacement windows and doors (since April 2002), removal of internal walls or chimney breasts, insulation, and any other structural or significant building work.

The Three Types of Documentation:

1. Planning Permission — Required when work changes the external appearance of the building. Some work is 'Permitted Development' and doesn't need it — but this doesn't mean no documentation is needed.

2. Building Regulations Approval — Required for structural works, extensions, conversions, and many alterations regardless of planning permission. A Completion Certificate is issued once works are signed off by an inspector.

3. Competent Person Certificates — Certain trades can self-certify: FENSA for windows, Gas Safe for gas, NAPIT or NICEIC for electrics. These replace the need for building control for that specific work.

If documentation is missing, your solicitor may recommend indemnity insurance to protect against the risk of enforcement.
BUYER_GUIDANCE: Building works without proper documentation carry real risks — the work may not meet required standards and the local authority could require remediation. If significant works are disclosed without corresponding approvals, your solicitor will advise on whether indemnity insurance is appropriate.
SELLER_GUIDANCE: Gather all paperwork for any works done during your ownership: planning permissions, building regulations completion certificates, competent person certificates, and any architect or structural engineer reports. If documents are lost, your solicitor can advise — sometimes they can be retrieved from the local authority.
KEY_POINTS:
• Disclose all significant building works — extensions, conversions, new windows, structural changes
• Three types of documentation may be needed: planning permission, building regulations approval, competent person certificates
• Permitted Development does not mean no documentation is needed
• Missing certificates can be covered by indemnity insurance in many cases

"Has any building work been done at your property? Extension? Loft conversion? New windows? Knocked through walls? This is one of the most important areas — and one where missing paperwork causes the most delays.

[THREE TYPES NEEDED]
Planning Permission — if the work changed the outside. Building Regulations Approval — about safety, needed for most structural work. Competent Person Certificates — self-certified by trades like FENSA or Gas Safe.

[FOR SELLERS]

[FOR BUYERS]

[CLOSE]

QUESTION: Does the property have solar panels or a solar power system?
SHORT_DESCRIPTION: Owned vs leased solar systems, Feed-in Tariffs, Smart Export Guarantee, and mortgage implications.
MAIN_EXPLANATION:
Two very different situations:

Situation 1 — You own the panels: You paid for them. They transfer with the property. Simpler for mortgage lenders.

Situation 2 — Provider-owned under a roof lease: A company installed the panels for free in exchange for a long lease of your roof or airspace (typically 20-25 years). The provider owns the panels — not you. A roof lease granted to a third party can affect the buyer's ability to get a mortgage.

Feed-in Tariff (FIT): Closed to new applicants since April 2019. Where panels were owner-paid, FIT payments can transfer to the buyer.

Smart Export Guarantee (SEG): The current scheme (launched January 2020) allowing surplus electricity to be sold back to the National Grid.

Information needed: who owns the panels, roof lease details, installation year, FIT or SEG agreement, battery storage details, and maintenance agreement.
BUYER_GUIDANCE: Tell your mortgage lender about any solar arrangement before proceeding. Find out whether they will lend on properties with a roof lease — some lenders won't. Check whether any FIT or SEG income transfers to you as the buyer.
SELLER_GUIDANCE: Contact your solar panel provider and gather: the original installation agreement, details of who owns the panels, any roof lease documentation, FIT or SEG agreement documents, your most recent electricity bill, and the MCS installation certificate.
KEY_POINTS:
• Owner-purchased panels: straightforward — transfer with the property
• Provider-installed under a roof lease: complex — can affect mortgage availability
• Disclose: ownership details, roof lease (if any), FIT/SEG agreements, MCS certificate
• Buyers must check with their mortgage lender before proceeding
VIDEO_SCRIPT_FULL:
[INTRO]
"Solar panels — increasingly common on UK homes and potentially complicated when selling.

[THE KEY QUESTION]
Two situations: you own the panels — simple. Or a company installed them for free and took a lease of your roof — complex.

[WHY THE ROOF LEASE MATTERS]
A company holding a lease on your roof can affect the buyer's mortgage. Some lenders won't lend on these arrangements.

[SELLERS]
Contact your solar provider. Get all paperwork: ownership details, any lease, FIT or SEG agreement, MCS certificate.

[BUYERS]
Tell your mortgage lender about any solar arrangement before you go further.

[CLOSE]
Solar panels: a great asset with paperwork that needs to be in order."
VIDEO_SCRIPT_SHORT: "Do you OWN your solar panels, or did a company install them for free and take a lease of your roof? These are very different situations. A roof lease can affect the buyer's mortgage — some lenders won't lend on it. Get your solar paperwork together before you sell. #SolarPanels #PropertySale"
DISCLAIMER: This content is for general educational purposes only — not legal advice. Always consult a qualified solicitor for guidance specific to your situation.

QUESTION: Is your property a listed building?
SHORT_DESCRIPTION: Listed building status, planning controls, consent requirements, and the enforcement risks for buyers and sellers.
MAIN_EXPLANATION:
A listed building is one officially recognised as having special architectural or historic interest.
• Grade I — Highest significance (around 2% of listed buildings)
• Grade II* — Particularly important (around 6%)
• Grade II — Nationally important (around 92%)

Key rules:
• Listed building consent is required for any alterations affecting the character of the building — inside AND outside
• Even replacing windows with identical-looking ones may require consent
• Enforcement for unauthorised works has NO TIME LIMIT
• A current owner can face enforcement for work done by a previous owner

Where to check:
• England: historicengland.org.uk/listing/the-list
• Wales: cadw.gov.wales
BUYER_GUIDANCE: Commission a specialist listed buildings survey — not just a standard homebuyer report. Ask your solicitor to check the history of any alterations and whether listed building consent was obtained. Understand that you are taking on legal responsibility for the building's listed status and all that entails.
SELLER_GUIDANCE: Declare your listed status. Think carefully about any works carried out during your ownership — was consent obtained for kitchen renovations, new windows, structural changes? If any work was done without consent, your solicitor needs to know.
KEY_POINTS:
• Listed building consent is needed for most changes — inside and outside
• No time limit on enforcement action — applies to all owners past and present
• A buyer can face action for work done by a previous owner
• Specialist listed buildings survey is strongly recommended for buyers
VIDEO_SCRIPT_FULL:
[INTRO]
"Is your property a listed building? Simple question, significant implications.

[WHAT LISTING MEANS]
Listed building consent is required for changes — inside or outside. Even certain repairs. And enforcement has no time limit — action can be taken decades after work was done, against the current owner.

[FOR SELLERS]
Think about any works done during your ownership. Was consent obtained? Tell your solicitor.

[FOR BUYERS]
Get a specialist listed buildings surveyor. Have your solicitor specifically investigate the history of any alterations.

[CLOSE]
Listed buildings are wonderful — but they come with serious responsibilities."
VIDEO_SCRIPT_SHORT: "Selling a listed building? Any alterations — inside or outside — needed listed building consent. Enforcement has NO time limit — a buyer could face action for work done by a previous owner. Disclose everything. #ListedBuilding #PropertySale"
DISCLAIMER: This content is for general educational purposes only — not legal advice. Always consult a qualified solicitor for guidance specific to your situation.

QUESTION: Are any trees on or near your property protected?
SHORT_DESCRIPTION: Tree Preservation Orders, conservation area tree protections, and owner obligations.
MAIN_EXPLANATION:
A Tree Preservation Order (TPO) is a legal protection placed on a specific tree by the local planning authority. Once a TPO is in place, it is an offence to cut down, top, lop, uproot, wilfully damage, or destroy a protected tree without the council's permission.

Conservation areas: Even without a specific TPO, trees in conservation areas have automatic protections. Before significant work, the owner must submit a formal notification to the council and wait six weeks.

What must be disclosed:
• Any trees on or overhanging the property subject to a TPO
• Any work carried out on protected trees — with documentation showing permission was obtained

TPOs can affect where you build — extensions, outbuildings, and hard landscaping near protected trees may face restrictions.
BUYER_GUIDANCE: Trees with TPOs are a responsibility that transfers with the property. You cannot remove or significantly alter a protected tree without council permission — even if it is causing structural damage. Factor tree management and any restrictions into your plans before exchange.
SELLER_GUIDANCE: If you are not sure whether any trees are protected, check with your local council. If you have had any work done on trees at the property, confirm that permission was obtained and provide any relevant documents.
KEY_POINTS:
• TPOs make it illegal to remove, top, lop, or damage a protected tree without council permission
• Conservation area trees have automatic protection even without a specific TPO
• TPO responsibilities transfer to the new owner with the property
• Tree restrictions can affect future planning applications for extensions or outbuildings
VIDEO_SCRIPT_FULL:
[INTRO]
"That tree in the garden might be legally protected — and that matters for buyer and seller alike.

[WHAT IS A TPO?]
A TPO placed by the local council protects a tree from being cut down, heavily pruned, or damaged without permission.

[CONSERVATION AREAS]
Trees in conservation areas are automatically protected even without a specific TPO.

[FOR SELLERS]
If you have had tree work done — confirm you had permission. Include any relevant documents.

[FOR BUYERS]
A protected tree is a responsibility — you can't make it go away because it's in the way. Factor it into your plans.

[CLOSE]
Trees with TPOs: not a dealbreaker, but a commitment."
VIDEO_SCRIPT_SHORT: "Protected tree on your property? You can't remove it, top it, or damage it without council permission. Conservation area? Same rules apply automatically. Disclose any TPOs and any work done on protected trees. #TreePreservationOrder #PropertySale"
DISCLAIMER: This content is for general educational purposes only — not legal advice. Always consult a qualified solicitor for guidance specific to your situation.

QUESTION: Has the property ever been flooded?
SHORT_DESCRIPTION: Flood history disclosure requirements, types of flooding, insurance implications, and independent checks.
MAIN_EXPLANATION:
You must disclose if any part of the property has ever been flooded — including the main building, garage, garden, or any outbuildings.

Even a single event must be disclosed — even if minor, even if only the garden, even if it happened before your ownership but you are aware of it.

Types of flooding:
• Surface water — heavy rainfall overwhelms drainage systems
• Groundwater — water table rises above ground level
• River flooding — a nearby watercourse overflows
• Coastal flooding — sea defences are breached
• Sewer flooding — sewers overflow

Flood defences installed at the property (barriers, sump pumps, air brick covers) must also be disclosed.

Failing to disclose known flooding is one of the most common grounds for post-completion compensation claims.
BUYER_GUIDANCE: Carry out independent checks at gov.uk/check-flooding and gov.uk/request-flooding-history. Speak to your buildings insurance broker BEFORE exchange — find out if the property can be insured and at what cost. Some flooded properties carry enormous premiums or are practically uninsurable.
SELLER_GUIDANCE: Be honest. State when flooding occurred, what type it was, and which parts were affected. Note any measures taken since. Your solicitor can help you phrase this appropriately.
KEY_POINTS:
• Disclose any flooding — however minor, however historic, however partial
• Includes garden flooding, not just the building
• Types: surface water, groundwater, river, coastal, sewer
• Buyers should check gov.uk/check-flooding independently before exchange
VIDEO_SCRIPT_FULL:
[INTRO]
"Flooding is one of the most serious issues a property can have — and one where sellers are most at risk if they don't disclose what they know.

[WHAT MUST BE DISCLOSED]
If any part of the property has ever flooded — house, garage, garden — you must say so. Even once. Even the garden only.

[THE CONSEQUENCES]
This is one of the most common reasons buyers claim compensation from sellers after completion.

[FOR BUYERS]
Use the Environment Agency's flood risk checker at gov.uk/check-flooding. And before exchange — call your buildings insurer to check whether the property can be insured.

[CLOSE]
Flooding: disclose it honestly. Check it independently."
VIDEO_SCRIPT_SHORT: "Has your property ever flooded — even just the garden, even just once? You MUST disclose it. Hiding flood history is a top reason buyers claim compensation from sellers. Buyers: check gov.uk/check-flooding independently. #FloodRisk #PropertySale"
DISCLAIMER: This content is for general educational purposes only — not legal advice. Always consult a qualified solicitor for guidance specific to your situation.

QUESTION: Has the property been tested for radon gas?
SHORT_DESCRIPTION: Radon gas — what it is, which areas are affected, test results, and remediation.
MAIN_EXPLANATION:
Radon is a naturally occurring radioactive gas that rises from rocks and soil. It is invisible, odourless, and undetectable without specialist equipment. Indoors it can accumulate, and at high levels it is a significant health risk — the second leading cause of lung cancer in the UK after smoking.

Areas at higher risk include parts of: Cornwall and Devon, Northamptonshire and Leicestershire, Derbyshire, Yorkshire, and parts of Wales.

Your solicitor's local authority search will usually indicate whether the property is in a designated Radon Affected Area.

Remediation typically involves: a sump system beneath the floor, improved underfloor ventilation, or sealing floors and walls. Costs are typically a few thousand pounds and are usually effective.

Information required:
• Has a radon test been carried out?
• Was the result below or above the recommended action level?
• Have any remedial measures been taken?
BUYER_GUIDANCE: If the property is in a Radon Affected Area, a radon test is strongly advisable. Test kits are available from around Ł50 and take a few months to produce a result. If the result is above the recommended action level, factor remediation costs into your budget.
SELLER_GUIDANCE: If a radon test has been carried out, share the results. If remediation has been carried out, disclose this and provide documentation — it is reassuring for buyers to know the issue has been addressed.
KEY_POINTS:
• Radon is a naturally occurring radioactive gas — the second biggest cause of lung cancer in the UK
• Certain areas of England and Wales have higher radon levels
• Disclose any test results and any remediation measures taken
• Test kits are inexpensive — buyers in affected areas should consider commissioning one
VIDEO_SCRIPT_FULL:
[INTRO]
"Radon — invisible, odourless, and the second biggest cause of lung cancer in the UK. If you're in the right postcode, it genuinely matters.

[WHAT IS IT?]
A radioactive gas from rocks and soil. Harmless outside. Can build up indoors to dangerous levels.

[WHERE IS IT A RISK?]
Cornwall, Devon, parts of the Midlands, Yorkshire, and Wales. Your solicitor's searches will flag whether you're in a Radon Affected Area.

[FOR SELLERS]
If a test has been done, share the results. Remediation documentation is actually reassuring, not alarming.

[FOR BUYERS]
In an affected area with no test done? Consider commissioning one. Test kits are inexpensive.

[CLOSE]
Radon is manageable — but only if you know it's there."
VIDEO_SCRIPT_SHORT: "Radon gas — invisible, odourless, second biggest cause of lung cancer in the UK. Sellers in Radon Affected Areas: disclose any tests and remediation. Buyers: check if you're in an affected area and consider commissioning a test. #RadonGas #PropertyHealth"
DISCLAIMER: This content is for general educational purposes only — not legal advice. Always consult a qualified solicitor for guidance specific to your situation.

QUESTION: Is the property affected by Japanese knotweed?
SHORT_DESCRIPTION: Japanese knotweed — what it is, why it matters, management plans, and mortgage implications.
MAIN_EXPLANATION:
Japanese knotweed is an invasive plant that can damage buildings, walls, hard surfaces, and drainage systems. It has distinctive bamboo-like stems and an underground root system (rhizome) that can penetrate building foundations.

A property is considered at risk if knotweed is growing within 3 metres of the boundary.

Why it is problematic:
• The underground rhizome can survive even after above-ground growth is treated
• Full treatment typically takes 3-5 years of professional management
• Can affect mortgage availability — some lenders refuse to lend on affected properties

Many sellers honestly answer 'not known' — and that is acceptable. Knotweed does not always show above ground, especially in winter.

A professional management and treatment plan is a structured programme of treatment usually backed by insurance. These plans are reassuring for buyers and lenders.
BUYER_GUIDANCE: If knotweed is disclosed: ask whether a professional management plan is in place and insured. Speak to your mortgage lender BEFORE exchange — some will lend with a plan in place, others will not lend until the property is clear. Factor ongoing management costs into your budget.
SELLER_GUIDANCE: If you are aware of knotweed, disclose it. If a management plan is in place, provide full documentation. If you answer 'not known,' that is acceptable where you genuinely do not know.
KEY_POINTS:
• Properties within 3 metres of Japanese knotweed are considered at risk
• The underground rhizome can survive treatment — full eradication takes years
• 'Not known' is an acceptable answer where you genuinely are not sure
• A professional management plan with insurance is reassuring for buyers and mortgage lenders
VIDEO_SCRIPT_FULL:
[INTRO]
"Japanese knotweed. Three words that make property buyers and sellers nervous. Let's separate fact from fear.

[WHAT IS IT?]
An invasive plant with a root system that can penetrate foundations and damage buildings. Properties within 3 metres of knotweed are at risk.

[FOR SELLERS]
If you are aware of knotweed, disclose it. If a management plan is in place, provide all the documentation — it's a positive thing, showing the problem is handled professionally.

[FOR BUYERS]
If knotweed is disclosed, your first call is to your mortgage lender. Some are fine with a managed plan; others won't lend until clear.

[CLOSE]
Japanese knotweed: manageable, but needs honest disclosure and proper handling."
VIDEO_SCRIPT_SHORT: "Japanese knotweed near your property? Disclose it — even if it's being managed. Buyers: is there a professional management plan? Tell your mortgage lender before exchange. Some lenders are fine with it. Others won't lend at all. #JapaneseKnotweed #PropertySale"
DISCLAIMER: This content is for general educational purposes only — not legal advice. Always consult a qualified solicitor for guidance specific to your situation.

QUESTION: Has any electrical installation work been carried out at the property?
SHORT_DESCRIPTION: Electrical compliance certificates, EICRs, and what buyers and sellers need to know.
MAIN_EXPLANATION:
Since January 2005, significant electrical installation work must comply with building regulations. Work that falls under this requirement includes:
• New circuit installations
• Replacement of consumer units or fuse boxes
• Rewiring in outbuildings and gardens
• Alterations to circuits in special locations (bathrooms, shower rooms, swimming pools)
• EV charging point installation

For work carried out after January 2005, one of the following is required:
1. BS7671 Electrical Installation Certificate — signed by the installer
2. Building Regulations Compliance Certificate — from the installer's Competent Person scheme (e.g. NAPIT or NICEIC)
3. Building Control Completion Certificate — from the local authority

The EICR (Electrical Installation Condition Report) assesses the safety and condition of all electrical installations. Required by law for rental properties (renewed every 5 years). Not legally required for property sales — but strongly recommended for older properties.
BUYER_GUIDANCE: For older properties (pre-1970s especially), consider commissioning an independent EICR. Old wiring can be a safety concern and may affect insurance. Check with your home insurer whether old wiring affects your policy.
SELLER_GUIDANCE: Find the relevant electrical certificates for any work done during your ownership. If you cannot find a certificate for recent work, contact the original electrician — they may be able to reissue it.
KEY_POINTS:
• Significant electrical work since January 2005 must have a compliance certificate
• One of three certificates is acceptable: BS7671, Building Regulations Compliance, or Building Control Completion
• An EICR is not legally required for sales but is recommended for older properties
• Missing certificates can often be reissued by the original electrician
VIDEO_SCRIPT_FULL:

"The electrics in a home are invisible — but one of the first things a thorough buyer will ask about.

[WHAT IS REQUIRED?]
Any significant electrical work since January 2005 needs a certificate — BS7671, Building Regulations Compliance, or Building Control Completion Certificate.

[THE EICR]
A full inspection of the electrical systems. Required for rental properties. Not required for sales — but strongly recommended for older homes.

[FOR SELLERS]
Find your electrical certificates. If you cannot find them, contact the original electrician.

[FOR BUYERS]
For older properties, consider paying for your own EICR. Old wiring can be a safety and insurance issue.

[CLOSE]
Electrics: hidden but crucial. Get the paperwork in order."
VIDEO_SCRIPT_SHORT: "Electrical work done since 2005 needs a certificate. No certificate? Tell your solicitor. For older properties, buyers should consider commissioning an EICR inspection. Old wiring can affect safety AND insurance. #ElectricalSafety #PropertySale"
DISCLAIMER: This content is for general educational purposes only — not legal advice. Always consult a qualified solicitor for guidance specific to your situation.

QUESTION: How is the property heated and when was the system last serviced?
SHORT_DESCRIPTION: Heating system types, installation certificates, service history, and what buyers need to assess.
MAIN_EXPLANATION:
Different heating systems require different documentation:
• Gas boiler (installed after April 2005): Building Regulations Completion Certificate from a Gas Safe registered engineer
• Oil boiler: Building Regulations Completion Certificate
• Air source heat pump: Building Regulations Completion Certificate (and possibly planning permission)
• Wood-burning stove: HETAS certificate or Building Regulations Completion Certificate
• Underfloor heating: Building Regulations Completion Certificate

Information required: type of heating, installation date, boiler installation date, compliance certificates, most recent service report, whether the system is currently working, and year of last service.

Boiler age matters: Boilers typically last 10-15 years. An older boiler approaching replacement can cost Ł2,000-Ł4,000 — relevant information for a buyer's budget.
BUYER_GUIDANCE: Key questions: how old is the boiler, when was it last serviced (should be annually), is it working, and are there compliance certificates? Consider having an independent Gas Safe or heating engineer inspect the system before exchange.
SELLER_GUIDANCE: Gather: the boiler installation certificate, annual service records, the most recent boiler inspection report, and certificates for any other heating systems. If missing service records, check with your service provider — they may have records.
KEY_POINTS:
• Gas boilers installed after April 2005 need a Gas Safe compliance certificate
• Boilers should be serviced annually — provide as many service records as available
• Boiler age matters: 10-15 year lifespan means an old boiler may need replacement soon
• Heat pumps and stoves also require specific installation certificates
VIDEO_SCRIPT_FULL:
[INTRO]
"The heating system is one of the most important — and potentially most expensive — things in any home.

[THE AGE QUESTION]
Buyers really want to know how old the boiler is. Boilers typically last 10-15 years. A 12-year-old boiler potentially means a Ł3,000 replacement in the near future.

[FOR SELLERS]
Gather: installation certificate, service records, and the most recent inspection report.

[FOR BUYERS]
Consider having an independent heating engineer check the system before exchange.

[CLOSE]
Heating: central to comfort and running costs. Be thorough."
VIDEO_SCRIPT_SHORT: "When was your boiler last serviced? How old is it? Boilers last 10-15 years — an old one is a potential Ł3,000+ bill for the buyer. Sellers: provide installation certificates and service records. Buyers: ask for a demonstration and consider an independent check. #BoilerService #PropertySale"

QUESTION: How is the property connected to drainage and sewerage?
SHORT_DESCRIPTION: Mains drainage, septic tanks, sewage treatment plants, cesspools, and compliance requirements.
MAIN_EXPLANATION:
Two scenarios:

Mains connected (most urban and suburban properties): Connected to the public sewer for both foul water (toilets, sinks, baths) and surface water (gutters, hard surfaces). No ongoing maintenance responsibility beyond the property boundary.

Private sewerage systems — three types:

Septic Tank: Solids settle, liquid flows out. Since January 2020 in England, cannot discharge directly to rivers, streams, or other surface water. Must discharge to a drainage field or mains sewer.

Sewage Treatment Plant: Treats waste to a higher standard. Can discharge to a watercourse in England if compliant with Environment Agency rules.

Cesspool: Completely sealed, no discharge. All waste must be emptied regularly by a registered waste carrier.

Information required for private systems: installation date, last service or emptying date, whether it discharges to ground or surface water, whether shared with neighbours, and whether any part is outside the property boundary.
BUYER_GUIDANCE: If the property has a private sewerage system: find out the annual servicing cost, confirm current Environment Agency compliance (especially for septic tanks previously discharging to surface water), and check whether any part is outside the property's boundaries. A non-compliant system can be expensive to upgrade.
SELLER_GUIDANCE: Know what system you have and provide accurate dates. If your septic tank still discharges to surface water, this must be disclosed and resolved — it has been non-compliant since January 2020.
KEY_POINTS:
• Most properties connect to the mains sewer — confirm this is the case
• Septic tanks in England cannot discharge to surface water since January 2020
• Private systems require ongoing maintenance and have compliance obligations
• Buyers should establish annual servicing costs and confirm current compliance
VIDEO_SCRIPT_FULL:
[INTRO]
"Drainage — most homeowners never think about it. But if your property has a private sewerage system, this is one of the most important things to get right when selling.

[THE THREE PRIVATE SYSTEMS]
Septic tank — liquid must go to a drainage field, not directly to rivers (illegal since 2020). Sewage treatment plant — processes waste to a higher standard. Cesspool — sealed tank, everything pumped out regularly.

[FOR BUYERS]
Find out annual servicing costs, who does it, and whether the system currently complies with Environment Agency rules.

[CLOSE]
Drainage: invisible, essential, and legally regulated."
VIDEO_SCRIPT_SHORT: "Does your property have a septic tank? Since 2020, they can't discharge directly to rivers or streams in England. Is yours compliant? Buyers: find out servicing costs and compliance status before exchange. Non-compliant systems can cost tens of thousands to fix. #SepticTank #PropertySale"
DISCLAIMER: This content is for general educational purposes only — not legal advice. Always consult a qualified solicitor for guidance specific to your situation.

QUESTION: Is the property leasehold? What does that actually mean?
SHORT_DESCRIPTION: Plain-English explanation of leasehold — how it differs from freehold and why lease length is critical.
MAIN_EXPLANATION:
Freehold: You own the land and the building outright. No landlord. No end date on your ownership.

Leasehold: You own the right to occupy the property for a fixed number of years. Someone else — the freeholder — owns the land and building. You have a landlord.

Most flats in England and Wales are leasehold. Some houses are too, though this is becoming less common.

Why lease length matters:
• 80+ years remaining: Generally fine for mortgages and future sale
• Under 80 years: Lease extension becomes significantly more expensive (marriage value applies)
• Under 70 years: Many lenders become cautious
• Under 60 years: Most lenders will not lend
• Under 40 years: Very difficult to sell or mortgage

Extending a lease: After owning the property for 2 years, you have a legal right to extend your lease by 90 years at zero (peppercorn) ground rent. Cost depends on the unexpired term, current ground rent, and property value.
BUYER_GUIDANCE: Before buying leasehold, confirm: how many years are left on the lease, what the ground rent is and whether it escalates, who manages the building and how well, what the service charges are, whether there is a healthy reserve fund, and whether there are any known building safety issues.
SELLER_GUIDANCE: Know your lease details: current unexpired term, annual ground rent and whether it escalates, service charge amounts for the last three years, and contact details for the freeholder and managing agent. If the lease has fewer than 80 years remaining, expect this to affect the sale.
KEY_POINTS:
• Leasehold means you own the right to occupy — not the building or land
• Lease length is critical: under 80 years makes extension expensive, under 70 concerns lenders
• You have a legal right to extend after 2 years of ownership
• Always check: years remaining, ground rent terms, service charges, management quality
VIDEO_SCRIPT_FULL:
[INTRO]
"Buying a flat in England or Wales? You are almost certainly buying leasehold. And leasehold is very different from owning a freehold house.

[THE SIMPLE EXPLANATION]
With a freehold house, you own the building and the land. With a leasehold flat, you own the right to live there for a set number of years. Someone else — the freeholder — still owns the building. You have a landlord.

[WHY LEASE LENGTH MATTERS]
Below 80 years, extending gets significantly more expensive. Below 70, lenders get nervous. Below 60, most lenders won't touch it.

[THE GOOD NEWS]
After 2 years of ownership, you have a legal right to extend your lease. It's a right, not a favour.

[CLOSE]
Leasehold isn't bad. But it's different. Make sure you understand what you're buying."
VIDEO_SCRIPT_SHORT: "Buying a leasehold flat? You have a landlord — the freeholder. Lease length MATTERS: under 80 years = expensive to extend. Under 70 = lenders get nervous. Under 60 = most lenders won't lend. Always check the lease term before you buy. #LeaseholdExplained #FlatBuying"
DISCLAIMER: This content is for general educational purposes only — not legal advice. Always consult a qualified solicitor for guidance specific to your situation.

QUESTION: What are ground rent and service charges — and how much should I expect to pay?
SHORT_DESCRIPTION: Ground rent types, escalating clauses, service charges, reserve funds, and Section 20 consultations.
MAIN_EXPLANATION:
Ground Rent: An annual payment from the leaseholder to the freeholder. New leases (post-2022) must have zero (peppercorn) ground rent. Older leases may have significant amounts.

Types of ground rent:
• Peppercorn: Effectively zero
• Fixed: Stays the same throughout the lease
• Escalating (RPI or doubling): Increases over time — doubling ground rents can make properties unmortgageable

Service Charges cover: building insurance, communal area maintenance, gardening, management fees, and reserve fund contributions.

The Reserve (Sinking) Fund: Well-managed buildings maintain a reserve fund for future major works. Without one, leaseholders face sudden 'special levies' — potentially Ł10,000-Ł30,000+ per flat.

Section 20 Consultations: For major works costing any leaseholder more than Ł250, the landlord must formally consult leaseholders. Any active or recent Section 20 notice must be disclosed.
BUYER_GUIDANCE: Calculate the full annual cost: ground rent (if applicable), annual service charge, and reserve fund contribution. Find out: Is the reserve fund healthy? Are any major works planned in the next 3-5 years? Is there an active Section 20 notice that could result in a large levy?
SELLER_GUIDANCE: Provide three years of service charge accounts. Be transparent about: the current annual service charge, any planned major works, any disputes about charges, and whether any service charges are currently outstanding.
KEY_POINTS:
• Ground rent — know if it escalates: doubling ground rents can make properties unmortgageable
• Service charges cover building running costs — verify current annual amount
• Reserve fund: no reserve = risk of sudden large special levies
• Request three years of service charge accounts and ask about any Section 20 notices
VIDEO_SCRIPT_FULL:
[INTRO]
"If you're buying a leasehold flat, there are two ongoing payments you need to understand: ground rent and service charges.

[GROUND RENT]
Ground rent is an annual payment to the freeholder. New leases are capped at zero. Older leases can have ground rents that escalate — doubling ground rents can make properties unmortgageable.

[SERVICE CHARGES]
Service charges pay for the upkeep of the whole building — cleaning, maintenance, insurance, management fees.

[THE RESERVE FUND]
Without a reserve fund, when the roof needs replacing, every leaseholder gets a special levy — sometimes Ł15,000 or more per flat. Before exchange, ask how much is in the reserve fund.

[CLOSE]
Ground rent and service charges: understand the numbers before you commit."
VIDEO_SCRIPT_SHORT: "Buying a leasehold flat? Check if ground rent escalates — doubling ground rent is a serious mortgage risk. Check the reserve fund — no reserve means potential surprise bills of thousands. Always ask for three years of service charge accounts. #LeaseholdCosts #ServiceCharges"
DISCLAIMER: This content is for general educational purposes only — not legal advice. Always consult a qualified solicitor for guidance specific to your situation.

QUESTION: Are there any building safety or cladding issues with the building?
SHORT_DESCRIPTION: Building Safety Act 2022, qualifying leases, leaseholder protections, and cladding transactions.
MAIN_EXPLANATION:
Background: Following the Grenfell Tower fire in 2017, thousands of residential buildings were found to have dangerous cladding or fire safety defects. The Building Safety Act 2022 created a legal framework to fund remediation and protect qualifying leaseholders from having to pay for defects they did not cause.

Key concepts:

Qualifying Lease: A lease meeting specific criteria under the Act. Qualifying leaseholders benefit from legal protections limiting their liability for remediation costs. Whether a lease qualifies depends on factors including whether the property was the leaseholder's main home on 14 February 2022.

Leaseholder Deed of Certificate: A formal legal document completed by the leaseholder confirming their qualifying status.

Landlord's Certificate: A certificate provided by the freeholder confirming details about the building and the landlord's financial position.

What must be disclosed: whether remediation works have been proposed or carried out, whether the lease is a qualifying lease, whether a Leaseholder Deed of Certificate has been completed, whether the freeholder has been notified of the intention to sell, and whether a Landlord's Certificate has been received.

This is a rapidly evolving area of law. Specialist legal advice is essential.
BUYER_GUIDANCE: Before exchange on any flat with potential cladding issues: request all documentation — Leaseholder Deed of Certificate, Landlord's Certificate, any remediation survey reports. Confirm with your mortgage lender whether they will lend on this property. Ensure your solicitor has specialist Building Safety Act expertise. Check whether the building is part of an approved remediation scheme.
SELLER_GUIDANCE: Complete your Leaseholder Deed of Certificate accurately before listing. Provide the Landlord's Certificate if received. Notify the freeholder of your intention to sell. Be fully transparent about any known issues. Your solicitor must have Building Safety Act expertise.
KEY_POINTS:
• The Building Safety Act 2022 protects qualifying leaseholders from paying for defects they didn't cause
• Two key documents: Leaseholder Deed of Certificate (completed by leaseholder) and Landlord's Certificate (from freeholder)
• Sellers must complete the Leaseholder Deed of Certificate before listing
• Buyers must check mortgage lender position before exchange — this is a specialist area of law
VIDEO_SCRIPT_FULL:
[INTRO]
"This might be the most important question in the whole process if you're buying or selling a flat in a block built after around 1990.

[THE BACKGROUND]
After Grenfell, the Building Safety Act 2022 was introduced to fix dangerous buildings and protect leaseholders from paying for defects that weren't their fault.

[THE KEY DOCUMENTS]
The Leaseholder Deed of Certificate — completed by you. The Landlord's Certificate — from the freeholder. Both are needed for the transaction.

[FOR SELLERS]
Complete your Leaseholder Deed of Certificate before you list. Notify the freeholder.

[FOR BUYERS]
Before you exchange, speak to your mortgage lender. Make sure your solicitor is specifically experienced in Building Safety Act matters.

[CLOSE]
Building safety is complex. Get specialist advice early."
VIDEO_SCRIPT_SHORT: "Buying a flat with cladding issues? The Building Safety Act 2022 protects qualifying leaseholders from paying for defects they didn't cause. You need: Leaseholder Deed of Certificate + Landlord's Certificate. Get specialist legal advice — don't skip this step. #BuildingSafety #CladdingCrisis"
DISCLAIMER: This content is for general educational purposes only — not legal advice. Always consult a qualified solicitor for guidance specific to your situation.

QUESTION: What items are included in the sale of the property?
SHORT_DESCRIPTION: The fittings and contents checklist, what sellers must declare, and avoiding moving-day disputes.
MAIN_EXPLANATION:
When a property changes hands, it must be absolutely clear what stays and what goes. This information can form part of the legal sale contract — sellers can be held to what they have committed to leaving.

For each item, sellers mark one of three options:
• Included — stays with the property
• Excluded — the seller is taking it
• None — the item does not exist at the property

If an excluded item is something the seller would sell separately, they can add a price.

Generally included (fixtures — attached to the building):
• Fitted kitchen units and integrated appliances
• Bathroom fittings (bath, basin, toilet)
• Built-in wardrobes, light fittings (unless excluded), door and window fittings
• Central heating system

Generally not included (chattels — movable items):
• Freestanding appliances, curtains, garden furniture, plants in pots

These are general rules — always check, never assume.
BUYER_GUIDANCE: Read the fittings and contents information carefully. Do not assume anything stays — verify it is marked as included. If something you saw during a viewing matters to you, confirm it is included before exchange. Any verbal agreements about what is staying must be reflected in the written information.
SELLER_GUIDANCE: Go through every category carefully. If unsure about an item, mark it excluded — you can always add it later. If taking something a buyer might reasonably expect to stay (like a designer light fitting), mark it excluded early so buyers have accurate expectations from viewings.
KEY_POINTS:
• The fittings and contents list can form part of the legal sale contract
• Mark every item: Included / Excluded / None — do not leave ambiguity
• Fixtures (attached to the building) legally stay; chattels (movable) legally go
• Verbal agreements mean nothing unless they appear in the written list
VIDEO_SCRIPT_FULL:
[INTRO]
"What stays in the house and what goes with you? It sounds simple. But it causes more moving-day arguments than almost anything else.

[HOW IT WORKS]
For every category of item, the seller marks: Included, Excluded, or None. This can form part of the legal sale contract.

[THE GREY AREA]
Things fixed to the building legally stay. Moveable things legally go. But that line causes disputes constantly.

[THE SOLUTION]
Be explicit. If you want to take something, mark it excluded — even if you think it's obvious.

[FOR BUYERS]
If something matters to you — the integrated appliance, the garden structure — make sure it is marked included before you exchange.

[CLOSE]
What stays, what goes: document it before contracts are exchanged."
VIDEO_SCRIPT_SHORT: "What's staying in the house and what goes with you? This must be documented — it becomes part of the legal contract. Mark EVERY item as included, excluded, or none. Buyers: don't assume anything stays. Read the list carefully. #FittingsAndContents #MovingDay"
DISCLAIMER: This content is for general educational purposes only — not legal advice. Always consult a qualified solicitor for guidance specific to your situation.

QUESTION: Can I take my light fittings when I move out?
SHORT_DESCRIPTION: The legal position on light fittings, the replacement obligation, and how to avoid the most common moving-day dispute.
MAIN_EXPLANATION:
The legal position: Light fittings are technically fixtures — attached to the electrical system and the fabric of the building. They legally stay with the property unless specifically agreed otherwise before exchange of contracts.

However, sellers are entitled to take light fittings provided they:
1. Declare them as excluded in the fittings information BEFORE exchange of contracts
2. Replace them with a basic fitting before completion

The replacement obligation: If you remove a light fitting, you MUST leave in its place:
• A ceiling rose
• A flex
• A bulb holder
• A bulb

This is both a legal contractual obligation and a health and safety requirement. Bare wires cannot be left.

How to avoid problems:
• Decide what you are taking before viewings begin
• Mark excluded fittings clearly before exchange
• Have basic replacements ready to install before completion day
BUYER_GUIDANCE: If a light fitting you expected to stay is not marked as included, flag this before exchange — not after. At the final inspection on completion morning, verify all excluded fittings have been properly replaced with basic alternatives. Bare wires are unacceptable and must be resolved before funds are released.
SELLER_GUIDANCE: Room by room: identify any fitting you want to take — designer pendants, chandeliers, specialist spotlights. Mark each as excluded before exchange. Source basic replacements and install them on or before completion day. If taking a fitting, having an electrician fit the replacement is the safest approach.
KEY_POINTS:
• Light fittings are fixtures and legally stay unless excluded before exchange
• If you take a fitting, you MUST replace it with: ceiling rose, flex, bulb holder, and bulb
• Leaving bare wires is a breach of contract AND a safety hazard
• Decide what you are taking before viewings — not after exchange
VIDEO_SCRIPT_FULL:
[INTRO]
"Light fittings — responsible for more moving-day disputes than almost anything else. Let's settle this once and for all.

[THE LEGAL POSITION]
Legally, light fittings are fixtures. They stay with the property unless you specifically agree otherwise in writing before exchange.

[CAN I TAKE MINE?]
Yes. But you must: mark it as excluded before exchange, and replace it with a basic ceiling rose, flex, bulb holder, and bulb. You cannot leave bare wires — that's a breach of contract and a safety hazard.

[THE TIMING]
Decide what you are taking before viewings — then buyers have accurate expectations from the start.

[CLOSE]
Light fittings: small detail, genuinely important. Get it documented early."

QUESTION: How much stamp duty will I pay?
SHORT_DESCRIPTION: Stamp Duty Land Tax rates, thresholds, first-time buyer relief, and additional property surcharge.
MAIN_EXPLANATION:
Stamp Duty Land Tax (SDLT) is a tax paid by the buyer when purchasing property in England and Northern Ireland. (Wales has Land Transaction Tax; Scotland has Land and Buildings Transaction Tax.) It must be paid within 14 days of completion.

Standard SDLT Rates in England (from April 2025):
• Up to Ł250,000: 0%
• Ł250,001 to Ł925,000: 5%
• Ł925,001 to Ł1,500,000: 10%
• Over Ł1,500,000: 12%

Rates apply to the portion of the price in each band — similar to income tax.
Example: Buying at Ł400,000 = 0% on first Ł250,000 + 5% on Ł150,000 = Ł7,500 SDLT.

First-Time Buyer Relief: No SDLT on the first Ł425,000 for properties up to Ł625,000. Standard rates apply above Ł625,000.

Additional Dwelling Surcharge: Buying while still owning another property — an additional 3% surcharge applies across all bands.

Note: SDLT rates and thresholds are set by government and can change. Always verify current rates with your solicitor.
BUYER_GUIDANCE: SDLT must be budgeted for alongside solicitor fees, surveyor fees, search fees, mortgage arrangement fees, removal costs, and any renovation costs. Your mortgage lender will NOT include SDLT in the amount they lend — it comes from your own funds. Use the HMRC SDLT calculator at gov.uk/stamp-duty-land-tax to calculate your specific figure.
KEY_POINTS:
• SDLT is paid by the buyer — not the seller
• Paid within 14 days of completion — solicitor usually handles this
• First-time buyers pay no SDLT on first Ł425,000 (for properties up to Ł625,000)
• Additional 3% surcharge applies when buying while already owning a property
• Always verify current rates — they can change with government budgets
VIDEO_SCRIPT_FULL:

[HOW IS IT CALCULATED?]
In bands — like income tax. As of April 2025: zero on the first Ł250,000, 5% on the portion up to Ł925,000, 10% up to Ł1.5 million, 12% above that.

On a Ł400,000 purchase: zero on the first Ł250,000, 5% on the remaining Ł150,000 = Ł7,500.

[FIRST-TIME BUYERS]
No stamp duty on the first Ł425,000 — as long as the property costs Ł625,000 or less.

[SECOND PROPERTIES]
Buying while owning another property? Add a 3% surcharge across all bands.

[CLOSE]
Budget for stamp duty alongside your deposit and solicitor fees. Use the HMRC calculator at gov.uk for your exact figure."

QUESTION: What help is available for first-time buyers?
SHORT_DESCRIPTION: First-time buyer stamp duty relief, Lifetime ISA, shared ownership, and other available schemes.
MAIN_EXPLANATION:
Who counts as a first-time buyer: Someone who has never previously owned a home anywhere in the world. In a joint purchase, ALL buyers must be first-time buyers to qualify.

Stamp Duty Relief: No SDLT on the first Ł425,000 (for properties up to Ł625,000).

Lifetime ISA (LISA): Open to anyone aged 18-39. Save up to Ł4,000 per year. Government adds a 25% bonus (up to Ł1,000/year). Can be used toward a first home purchase (property must be Ł450,000 or less). Must have held the LISA for at least 12 months before use.

Shared Ownership: Buy a share (typically 10-75%) from a housing association and pay rent on the remaining share. Option to buy more shares over time (staircasing) up to 100%.

Mortgage Guarantee Scheme: Government scheme allowing buyers to purchase with a 5% deposit on properties up to Ł600,000. Available from participating lenders.

First Homes Scheme: Newly built homes sold at a discount of at least 30% below market value. Discount stays with the property on future sales.

Schemes and eligibility change — always verify current details.
BUYER_GUIDANCE: Open a Lifetime ISA as early as possible — the 12-month minimum holding period means you cannot use it immediately. For shared ownership, understand the ongoing rent and service charge obligations before committing. Work with a qualified independent mortgage broker who can assess all options for your specific situation.
KEY_POINTS:
• All buyers in a joint purchase must be first-time buyers to qualify for relief
• Lifetime ISA: save Ł4k/year, get Ł1k/year government bonus — open one early
• Shared ownership: buy a share, pay rent on the rest — suitable where full ownership is unaffordable
• Mortgage Guarantee Scheme: 5% deposit mortgages on properties up to Ł600,000
• Schemes change — always verify current eligibility with your mortgage broker

QUESTION: What is remortgaging and when should I consider it?
SHORT_DESCRIPTION: What remortgaging is, when to do it, different types, and the costs involved.
MAIN_EXPLANATION:
Remortgaging means switching your existing mortgage — either to a new deal with the same lender, or to a completely new lender. You are not moving home; you are changing how your existing home is financed.

Common reasons to remortgage:
• End of fixed rate period: When a fixed-rate deal ends, you typically roll onto the lender's standard variable rate (SVR) — usually significantly more expensive
• To release equity: If the property has increased in value, remortgaging can release cash
• To reduce monthly payments: Switching to a lower rate or extending the mortgage term
• Improved loan-to-value: If your LTV has fallen, you may now qualify for better rates

Costs to consider:
• Early Repayment Charge (ERC): Penalty for leaving a fixed deal early — typically 1-5% of outstanding balance
• New arrangement fee: Some products charge a fee (can be added to the loan)
• Solicitor fees: Required if switching lender
• Valuation fee: Usually required by the new lender

Key tip: Start looking 3-6 months before your current deal expires.
BUYER_GUIDANCE: Use an independent mortgage broker — they can search the whole market, not just one lender. Check your Early Repayment Charge before switching mid-deal. Consider a product transfer — if your current lender offers a competitive rate, this is faster and may not need a solicitor. Maintain your credit score during the process.
KEY_POINTS:
• Remortgaging = switching your mortgage deal, not moving home
• Start looking 3-6 months before your current deal expires — avoid rolling onto the SVR
• Check your Early Repayment Charge before switching mid-deal
• Use an independent mortgage broker to search the whole market
• Product transfer with your current lender can be faster if their rate is competitive
VIDEO_SCRIPT_FULL:

[CLOSE]

QUESTION: How long does buying or selling a home actually take?
SHORT_DESCRIPTION: The conveyancing timeline from offer acceptance to completion, with typical durations for each stage.
MAIN_EXPLANATION:
Conveyancing is the legal process of transferring property ownership. In England and Wales, it typically takes 8-16 weeks from offer acceptance to completion.

Typical timeline:
• Weeks 1-3: Both parties instruct solicitors; seller's solicitor prepares title documents and property information
• Weeks 2-4: Buyer's solicitor orders searches (local authority, drainage, environmental)
• Weeks 3-6: Buyer's solicitor raises enquiries; seller's solicitor responds
• Weeks 4-8: Buyer's lender values the property and issues a formal mortgage offer
• Weeks 2-5: Buyer's surveyor inspects the property
• Weeks 8-16: Exchange of contracts — legally binding, deposit paid, completion date agreed
• 1-4 weeks after exchange: Completion — money transfers, keys released, ownership transfers

What causes delays: slow responses to enquiries, missing documents, long chains, survey issues requiring renegotiation, mortgage complications, and local authority search delays.

How to keep things moving: respond within 24-48 hours, gather documents early, instruct a mortgage broker alongside a solicitor, and be decisive.
BUYER_GUIDANCE: Do not hand in notice on your rental property until AFTER exchange of contracts — not when the offer is accepted. Build buffer time around planned moving dates. Book removals and arrange utility transfers in the gap between exchange and completion.
SELLER_GUIDANCE: The faster you complete and return your property information, the sooner the contract pack is issued. Gather planning permissions, guarantees, and certificates before or at the point of instruction — not when asked.
KEY_POINTS:
• Typical timeline: 8-16 weeks from offer acceptance to completion
• Exchange = legally binding; Completion = ownership transfers (usually 1-4 weeks apart)
• The single most important thing: respond to your solicitor within 24-48 hours
• Every delay you cause multiplies through the whole chain
VIDEO_SCRIPT_FULL:

Respond to your solicitor quickly. Every delay you cause multiplies through the chain.

[CLOSE]

Biggest tip: respond to your solicitor fast — every delay you cause multiplies through the whole chain. #Conveyancing #PropertyTimeline"

QUESTION: What's the difference between exchange and completion?
SHORT_DESCRIPTION: Exchange of contracts and completion — what each means, when they happen, and what the gap involves.
MAIN_EXPLANATION:
Exchange of Contracts: The sale becomes legally binding on both parties.

What happens at exchange:
• Both solicitors confirm contracts have been signed
• The buyer pays the deposit (typically 10% of the purchase price)
• A completion date is agreed and fixed
• Neither party can pull out without significant financial penalty

After exchange: The deal is legally done. Book removals. Give notice at work.

Completion: Ownership legally transfers and keys are handed over.

What happens at completion:
• The buyer's solicitor transfers the full purchase price to the seller's solicitor
• The seller's solicitor confirms receipt and authorises key release
• The buyer is now the legal owner
• The property is registered in the buyer's name at HM Land Registry

The gap between exchange and completion: Usually 1-4 weeks. In a chain, all properties must complete on the same day.

Critical: Standard sale contracts say risk passes to the buyer from exchange. If the property is seriously damaged between exchange and completion, the buyer bears the consequences. Buyers must arrange buildings insurance from exchange day — not completion.
BUYER_GUIDANCE: From exchange: you are legally committed — backing out means losing your deposit. Arrange buildings insurance immediately from exchange. On completion day: do your final inspection in the morning before funds are released. Do not hand in notice on a tenancy until after exchange.
SELLER_GUIDANCE: From exchange: keep your buildings insurance in place until completion day. On completion day: leave the property clean and completely empty, leave ALL keys at the property or with the estate agent, and leave alarm codes and instructions for any equipment.
KEY_POINTS:
• Exchange = legally binding on both parties; deposit paid; completion date fixed
• Completion = money transfers, keys handed over, ownership transfers
• Gap between exchange and completion: usually 1-4 weeks
• Buyers: arrange buildings insurance from EXCHANGE day — risk passes to you at that point
• Sellers: keep insurance running until completion day
VIDEO_SCRIPT_FULL:
[INTRO]
"Exchange and completion. Two words you'll hear constantly. And they're not the same thing.

[EXCHANGE]
When contracts are exchanged, the sale becomes legally binding. The buyer pays a deposit — typically 10%. A completion date is agreed. Neither side can walk away without serious consequences.

[COMPLETION]
The actual handover. The buyer's solicitor transfers the money. Keys are released. You are now the legal owner.

[THE GAP]
Usually 1-4 weeks between exchange and completion. Buyers must arrange buildings insurance from the date of EXCHANGE — standard contracts put risk of damage on the buyer from that point.

[CLOSE]
Exchange: legally committed. Completion: legally yours. Two different moments. Both matter."
VIDEO_SCRIPT_SHORT: "Exchange and completion — not the same thing! Exchange = legally binding, deposit paid. Completion = ownership transfers, keys in hand. Buyers: arrange buildings insurance from EXCHANGE, not completion — risk passes to you at that point. #ExchangeAndCompletion #PropertyBuying"

QUESTION: What are property searches and why do I need them?
SHORT_DESCRIPTION: The main property searches ordered by solicitors, what each covers, and why they matter.
MAIN_EXPLANATION:
Property searches are formal enquiries submitted by your solicitor to official bodies to check for issues that might affect the property. They are ordered before exchange of contracts.

The main searches:

Local Authority Search: The most important. Checks the council's records for: planning permissions (granted or refused), enforcement notices for unauthorised works, road adoption status, tree preservation orders, proposed road schemes, conservation area and listed building status, and compulsory purchase proposals.

Drainage and Water Search: Confirms whether the property connects to public sewers and whether any public sewers run within the property's boundaries (affects building works).

Environmental Search: Checks for contaminated land, flood risk, historic land use, ground stability issues, and proximity to landfill sites or radon affected areas.

Chancel Repair Liability Search: Checks whether the property carries a historic obligation to contribute to the repair of the chancel of a local medieval church.

Optional searches depending on location: coal mining, tin mining (Cornwall and Devon), HS2, detailed flood report.

Timing: Most searches return in 2-6 weeks. Cost: usually Ł200-Ł500 in total.
KEY_POINTS:
• Local authority search: planning, enforcement, road status, TPOs, compulsory purchase
• Drainage search: sewer connections and whether public sewers cross the property
• Environmental search: contamination, flood risk, historic land use
• Chancel repair: an obscure but real historic liability in some areas
• Total cost: usually Ł200-Ł500; timing: 2-6 weeks for most searches
VIDEO_SCRIPT_FULL:

[THE ENVIRONMENTAL SEARCH]

[CHANCEL REPAIR]
Obscure but real. In some areas, property owners have a historic liability to contribute to repairing a local medieval church.

[CLOSE]

QUESTION: Do I need a survey and which type should I get?
SHORT_DESCRIPTION: The three types of RICS survey, what each covers, and which is appropriate for different properties.
MAIN_EXPLANATION:
The lender's valuation is NOT a survey: When your mortgage lender arranges a valuation, it confirms the property is worth what you are paying — for the lender's benefit, not yours. It does not assess physical condition. You need your own independent survey.

The three main types:

RICS Level 1 — Condition Report: Basic traffic light ratings. No detailed recommendations. Best for: new builds or recently renovated properties in excellent condition.

RICS Level 2 — HomeBuyer Report: The most popular choice. Thorough inspection of accessible areas. Identifies significant defects and recommends further investigation. May include a market valuation. Best for: standard properties built after approximately 1900 in reasonable condition. Approximate cost: Ł400-Ł600.

RICS Level 3 — Building Survey (formerly Full Structural Survey): The comprehensive option. Detailed inspection of all accessible areas. In-depth analysis of construction, materials, and condition. Best for: properties built before 1900, unusual constructions, listed buildings, or properties requiring significant renovation. Approximate cost: Ł600-Ł1,500+.

When in doubt: upgrade to the Level 3 Building Survey. The cost difference is modest compared to the risk of missing a major defect.
KEY_POINTS:
• The mortgage lender's valuation is NOT your survey — it's for the lender's benefit only
• Level 1 Condition Report: for new builds and recently renovated properties
• Level 2 HomeBuyer Report: most popular — suitable for most standard post-1900 properties
• Level 3 Building Survey: for older, unusual, listed properties or significant renovation plans
• When in doubt, upgrade to Level 3 — cost difference is modest vs the risk
VIDEO_SCRIPT_FULL:

"Should you get a survey? Yes. What type? Let's work through it.

[THE LENDER'S VALUATION]
The lender's valuation is not your survey. It confirms the property is worth the loan — not whether the roof is about to fail or there is structural movement.

[THREE TYPES]
Level 1: Condition Report — for new builds. Level 2: HomeBuyer Report — for most standard properties. Level 3: Building Survey — for older, unusual, or listed properties, or anything you plan to substantially renovate.

[THE COST QUESTION]
A HomeBuyer Report might cost Ł500. A Building Survey might cost Ł900. A structural problem costing Ł30,000 to fix makes that gap look very different.

[CLOSE]
Get a survey. Choose the right level. Money extremely well spent."

For older or unusual properties: Level 3 Building Survey. A Ł500 survey could save you from a Ł30,000 surprise. Don't skip it. #PropertySurvey #HomeBuying"

QUESTION: What is indemnity insurance and do I need it?
SHORT_DESCRIPTION: Property indemnity insurance — what it covers, when it's used, what it costs, and its limitations.
MAIN_EXPLANATION:
Indemnity insurance is a one-off insurance policy that protects against the financial consequences of a specific legal issue in a property's history. It does NOT fix the underlying problem — it insures against the cost if the issue is ever enforced.

Common situations where it is used:
• Building works without planning permission
• Missing building regulations completion certificates
• Missing FENSA certificates for replacement windows
• Missing Gas Safe certificates for boiler installations
• Breach of a restrictive covenant
• Unclear or undocumented rights of way

Key facts:
• One-off premium — paid once; policy lasts indefinitely and usually passes to future owners
• Cost: typically Ł100-Ł400 for standard issues, more for complex situations
• The insured risk must NOT be disclosed to the relevant authority — doing so can invalidate the policy
• It does not grant the right to keep doing the thing — it covers the financial cost if ever enforced

Who pays: typically negotiated — if the issue predates the current seller, sellers usually pay.
BUYER_GUIDANCE: Indemnity insurance protects your finances, not your right to keep a structure in place. If a local authority orders an extension demolished, the policy covers the cost — but you still lose the extension. Understand the nature of the underlying risk before accepting an indemnity solution.
SELLER_GUIDANCE: If you have a known documentation issue, your solicitor may suggest indemnity insurance. Critically: once a policy is in place, do NOT raise the issue with the local authority or anyone who could enforce against it — this would be likely to invalidate the policy.
KEY_POINTS:
• Indemnity insurance covers the financial consequences of a specific legal issue
• It does NOT fix the problem — it pays out if the issue is ever enforced
• One-off premium, typically Ł100-Ł400 — policy lasts indefinitely
• Once in place: do NOT disclose the underlying issue to relevant authorities
• Seller usually pays where the issue predates their ownership
VIDEO_SCRIPT_FULL:
[INTRO]
"At some point in your transaction, you might hear the words indemnity insurance. What is it and should you be worried?

[WHAT IT IS]
A one-off policy covering the financial risk of a specific legal issue. Common examples: an extension without planning permission, a boiler without a Gas Safe certificate.

[WHAT IT DOES AND DOESN'T DO]
It covers the cost if the issue is ever enforced. It doesn't fix the underlying problem.

[THE GOLDEN RULE]
Once a policy is in place, do not draw attention to the underlying issue. Do not write to the council. Doing so could invalidate the cover.

[CLOSE]
Indemnity insurance: a practical solution to imperfect property history. Not alarming — but understand it fully."
